questions.text: Select the option which fits appropriately in the blank:It was so quiet you _ ( can) hear a pin drop.

options.explanation: The present tense "can" doesn't fit the context of the sentence which describes a past situation.

"Shall" is used for future tense or obligations which doesn't fit this sentence's context.
"Will" is used for future tense or intentions which doesn't fit this sentence context.

This is the present perfect tense which indicates that Bilal lived with his brother at some point in the past and may not still be living with him.
This is the past perfect tense which indicates that Bilal had lived with his brother before something else happened in the past.

questions.explanation: Facts that are always true do not need to be located in a specific time so they are stated in the present simple. Bilal lives with his brother.

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because it uses the indefinite article "a" which is appropriate when referring to a non-specific person or thing.

This option uses the definite article "the" which is appropriate when referring to a specific person or thing, previously mentioned or easily identifiable.
This option is incorrect because it omits any article, which is incorrect because an article is required to indicate whether the noun is specific or general.

questions.explanation: The definite article (the) is used before a noun to indicate that the identity of the noun is known to the reader.I think the man over there is very ill. He can’t stand on his feet.

questions.text: Demonstrate the control of tenses and sentence structure:Last Saturday my father _ (take) my friends and me to the circus.

options.explanation: Take is the first form of the verb that is used in the present tense hence it is an incorrect option.

Took is the most grammatically and tense wise correct option in the given statement. The key word in the statement is ‘Last Saturday’ explaining that the action took place in the far past hence the use of ‘took’.

Will/Shall + 1st form of the verb is used to indicate the simple future tense. For Example: Next Saturday, my father will take my friends and me to the circus. Hence, this is also the incorrect option.

Has taken is used in Present Perfect tense. This tense is used for past actions that are related to or continue in the present. The term "Last Saturday" is present, which means that the action has already ended in the past as well. Therefore, the last option is also incorrect.

questions.explanation: When mentioning an action that happened in the past, the past tense is used, along with the second form of the verb. Since the father took the children to the circus "Last Saturday" it indicates past tense.

questions.text: Demonstrate control of tenses and sentence structure.It _ (rained) since last night, and it looks as if it may rain all day.

options.explanation: Raining: This option is incorrect because it is in the present participle form (-ing form).which typically represents ongoing or continuous actions. However, the sentence indicates thatThe rain started in the past and is still ongoing. Therefore, a different verb form is needed toconvey this meaning.

Is raining? This option is also incorrect because it is in the present continuous tense.indicating an action happening at the moment. However, the sentence mentions that theRain started in the past and is still ongoing. The use of the present continuous tense does notalign with the time frame described in the sentence.

The correct answer is it has been raining".The sentence is in the present perfect continuous tense, which is used to describe an actionthat started in the past and is still continuing in the present. In this case, the action is "raining".The correct form of the verb to use in the present perfect continuous tense is "has/have been."present participle (the -ing form of the verb).

Rained: This option is incorrect as well because it is in the simple past tense, indicating a completed action in the past. However, the sentence suggests that the rain started in the past and is still ongoing. The use of the simple past tense does not convey the ongoing nature of the rain.

questions.explanation: The perfect present form of rain, i.e., "has been raining,” will be used, as the fact that it has been raining is an action that occurred in the past and is given importance in the present.

questions.text: Demonstrate the correct use of subject-verb agreement:Each and every member _ to vote.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: The correct verb form to use in the sentence "Each and every member _ to vote" is has.The subject of the sentence is "Each and every member," which is a compound subject. A compound subject is two or more subjects joined by the conjunction "and" or "or." When the subject of a sentence is a compound subject, the verb must agree with the plural form of the subject. In this case, the plural form of the subject is "members." Therefore, the verb must be in the plural form, which is "has."

Have: The verb "have" is in the singular form. It would be correct if the subject of the sentence were singular, such as "Each and every member has the right to vote." However, the subject of the sentence is plural, so the verb must be plural as well.

Having: The verb "having" is in the present participle form. It is used to form the progressive tenses, such as "is having" and "are being." However, the sentence is not in the progressive tense, so the present participle form of the verb is not correct.

Are: The verb "are" is in the present tense, plural form. It would be correct if the subject of the sentence were plural and the verb were in the present tense, such as "Each and every member are voting now." However, the verb in the sentence is in the infinitive form, so the present tense form of the verb is not correct.

questions.explanation: The correct verb form to use in the sentence "Each and every member _ to vote" is has. The subject of the sentence is "Each and every member," which is a compound subject. A compound subject is two or more subjects joined by the conjunction "and" or "or." When the subject of a sentence is a compound subject, the verb must agree with the plural form of the subject. In this case, the plural form of the subject is "members." Therefore, the verb must be in the plural form, which is "has."

Additionally, 'between' is the correct preposition to use when discussing two objects, in this case, his two fingers.Options B and C are incorrect because they both use the past tense 'ran', which does not follow the rule for the verb form after 'let'. Thus, Options B, C, and D do not adhere to the rules of subject-verb agreement or prepositional use.
